content.problemStatement:
Chef has hosted a party on Valentine's day for his single friends. He invited KKK single friends to the party. He initially had NNN chocolates in his apron which he decided to give away to his friends at the party.



Chef does not want to disappoint any of his friends. So he will distribute chocolates evenly to all his friends. If he has less than KKK chocolates, he will keep them to himself.


Since Chef is busy with the party arrangements, he asked you to calculate the number of chocolates each friend will get and the number of chocolates Chef will be left with after distribution.


Input Format
The first line of the input contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case will consist of 2 space separated integers, NNN and KKK denoting the number of chocolates and the number of friends as described above.



Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing two space separated integers, the first one being the number of chocolates each friend will get, followed by the number of chocolates left with the Chef.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and K, where N is the number of chocolates Chef has and K is the number of single friends invited.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with two space‑separated integers: the number of chocolates each friend receives, followed by the number of chocolates Chef keeps.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: 10 chocolates, 2 friends → each gets 10/2 = 5, remainder 0.\nTest case 2: 100 chocolates, 3 friends → each gets floor(100/3) = 33, remainder 1.
There are no friends (K = 0), so Chef keeps all chocolates.
Number of chocolates is less than number of friends, so each friend gets 0 and Chef keeps all 3.

solution.approach: For each test case we need to distribute chocolates evenly. If K is zero or N is smaller than K, no friend can receive a chocolate, so each friend gets 0 and Chef keeps all N chocolates. Otherwise each friend receives floor(N / K) chocolates and Chef keeps the remainder N % K. The operations are O(1) per test case and fit in 64‑bit unsigned integers.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
